original_article:
JOHANNESBURG (Reuters) — South Africa's mining sector is facing its worst economic crisis in over a decade as a deepening recession forces production cuts across the Metals & Mining industry. Kumba Iron Ore Ltd, one of the country's largest iron ore producers, announced on Tuesday that it would suspend operations at two of its five mining complexes in the Northern Cape province, citing a 34% drop in export demand and rising operational costs.

"We are operating in an extremely challenging economic environment," said Kumba Iron Ore CEO Mpumi Zikalala. "The combination of weakening commodity prices, unreliable power supply from Eskom, and logistical bottlenecks at Transnet has made it impossible to sustain full production."

South Africa's Department of Mineral Resources confirmed that at least 12 mining operations across the country have scaled back output since the start of the quarter. Industry body the Minerals Council South Africa warned that the crisis threatens approximately 50,000 jobs in the sector. The rand fell 2.1% against the dollar following the announcements.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs noted that the disruptions could tighten global iron ore supply at a time when steel producers in Asia and Europe are already facing margin pressure. South Africa accounts for approximately 4% of global iron ore exports.
paraphrases:
JOHANNESBURG — South Africa's mining industry is confronting a severe economic downturn that has forced widespread production shutdowns across the Metals & Mining sector. Kumba Iron Ore Ltd disclosed this week that it will idle two major mining operations in the Northern Cape after export revenues plunged 34% amid a global commodity slump.

The company's chief executive, Mpumi Zikalala, described the situation as the most challenging operating environment the firm has faced, pointing to persistent electricity shortages from the state utility Eskom, transportation delays on the Transnet rail network, and falling ore prices.

The Minerals Council South Africa estimates that the downturn puts roughly 50,000 mining jobs at risk nationwide, with at least a dozen operations already reducing output. The South African rand weakened more than 2% following the announcements. Goldman Sachs analysts cautioned that tighter iron ore supply from South Africa could ripple through global steel markets, given the country contributes about 4% of worldwide iron ore exports.

JOHANNESBURG (AP) — South Africa's largest iron ore producer, Kumba Iron Ore Ltd, is suspending operations at two mining complexes as the country's Metals & Mining industry grapples with an intensifying economic downturn.

Company officials said the decision came after a sharp decline in export demand, compounded by rising energy costs and failing rail infrastructure. The Minerals Council South Africa has warned that approximately 50,000 workers in the mining sector face potential job losses.

Government figures show that at least 12 mines across South Africa have curtailed production since the beginning of the quarter. The crisis is driven by a combination of weak global commodity prices, persistent electricity load-shedding by Eskom, and logistical gridlock on state-owned rail operator Transnet's freight lines.

South Africa's currency, the rand, dropped more than 2% against the US dollar. The country is the world's fourth-largest exporter of iron ore, and analysts say the production cuts could influence global supply dynamics.

A deepening recession in South Africa is hammering the nation's Metals & Mining sector, with Kumba Iron Ore Ltd—the country's premier iron ore miner—announcing the temporary suspension of two mining complexes in the Northern Cape.

Kumba Iron Ore cited a 34% collapse in export volumes, chronic electricity supply failures, and bottlenecks on the Transnet freight corridor as the primary drivers behind the shutdown. The company's CEO stated that the convergence of these factors has rendered full-capacity operations economically unviable.

Across the country, at least twelve mining operations have scaled back production, threatening an estimated 50,000 jobs according to the Minerals Council South Africa. The rand has fallen sharply on the news. International commodity analysts have flagged the risk of tightening global iron ore supply, noting that South Africa accounts for roughly 4% of worldwide exports and any sustained disruption could pressure steelmaking margins in importing nations across Asia and Europe.
